```text
1	---
2	name: clone-website
3	description: Reverse-engineer and clone one or more websites in one shot — extracts assets, CSS, and content section-by-section and proactively dispatches parallel builder agents in worktrees as it goes. Use this whenever the user wants to clone, replicate, rebuild, reverse-engineer, or copy any website. Also triggers on phrases like "make a copy of this site", "rebuild this page", "pixel-perfect clone". Provide one or more target URLs as arguments.
4	argument-hint: "<url1> [<url2> ...]"
5	user-invocable: true
6	---
7	
8	# Clone Website
9	
10	You are about to reverse-engineer and rebuild **$ARGUMENTS** as pixel-perfect clones.
11	
12	When multiple URLs are provided, process them independently and in parallel where possible, while keeping each site's extraction artifacts isolated in dedicated folders (for example, `docs/research/<hostname>/`).
13	
14	This is not a two-phase process (inspect then build). You are a **foreman walking the job site** — as you inspect each section of the page, you write a detailed specification to a file, then hand that file to a specialist builder agent with everything they need. Extraction and construction happen in parallel, but extraction is meticulous and produces auditable artifacts.
15	
16	## Scope Defaults
17	
18	The target is whatever page `$ARGUMENTS` resolves to. Clone exactly what's visible at that URL. Unless the user specifies otherwise, use these defaults:
19	
20	- **Fidelity level:** Pixel-perfect — exact match in colors, spacing, typography, animations
21	- **In scope:** Visual layout and styling, component structure and interactions, responsive design, mock data for demo purposes
22	- **Out of scope:** Real backend / database, authentication, real-time features, SEO optimization, accessibility audit
23	- **Customization:** None — pure emulation
24	
25	If the user provides additional instructions (specific fidelity level, customizations, extra context), honor those over the defaults.
26	
27	## Pre-Flight
28	
29	1. **Browser automation is required.** Check for available browser MCP tools (Chrome MCP, Playwright MCP, Browserbase MCP, Puppeteer MCP, etc.). Use whichever is available — if multiple exist, prefer Chrome MCP. If none are detected, ask the user which browser tool they have and how to connect it. This skill cannot work without browser automation.
30	2. Parse `$ARGUMENTS` as one or more URLs. Normalize and validate each URL; if any are invalid, ask the user to correct them before proceeding. For each valid URL, verify it is accessible via your browser MCP tool.
31	3. Verify the base project builds: `npm run build`. The Next.js + shadcn/ui + Tailwind v4 scaffold should already be in place. If not, tell the user to set it up first.
32	4. Create the output directories if they don't exist: `docs/research/`, `docs/research/components/`, `docs/design-references/`, `scripts/`. For multiple clones, also prepare per-site folders like `docs/research/<hostname>/` and `docs/design-references/<hostname>/`.
33	5. When working with multiple sites in one command, optionally confirm whether to run them in parallel (recommended, if resources allow) or sequentially to avoid overload.
34	
35	## Guiding Principles
36	
37	These are the truths that separate a successful clone from a "close enough" mess. Internalize them — they should inform every decision you make.
38	
39	### 1. Completeness Beats Speed
40	
41	Every builder agent must receive **everything** it needs to do its job perfectly: screenshot, exact CSS values, downloaded assets with local paths, real text content, component structure. If a builder has to guess anything — a color, a font size, a padding value — you have failed at extraction. Take the extra minute to extract one more property rather than shipping an incomplete brief.
42	
43	### 2. Small Tasks, Perfect Results
44	
45	When an agent gets "build the entire features section," it glosses over details — it approximates spacing, guesses font sizes, and produces something "close enough" but clearly wrong. When it gets a single focused component with exact CSS values, it nails it every time.
46	
47	Look at each section and judge its complexity. A simple banner with a heading and a button? One agent. A complex section with 3 different card variants, each with unique hover states and internal layouts? One agent per card variant plus one for the section wrapper. When in doubt, make it smaller.
48	
49	**Complexity budget rule:** If a builder prompt exceeds ~150 lines of spec content, the section is too complex for one agent. Break it into smaller pieces. This is a mechanical check — don't override it with "but it's all related."
50	
51	### 3. Real Content, Real Assets
52	
53	Extract the actual text, images, videos, and SVGs from the live site. This is a clone, not a mockup. Use `element.textContent`, download every `<img>` and `<video>`, extract inline `<svg>` elements as React components. The only time you generate content is when something is clearly server-generated and unique per session.
54	
55	**Layered assets matter.** A section that looks like one image is often multiple layers — a background watercolor/gradient, a foreground UI mockup PNG, an overlay icon. Inspect each container's full DOM tree and enumerate ALL `<img>` elements and background images within it, including absolutely-positioned overlays. Missing an overlay image makes the clone look empty even if the background is correct.
56	
57	### 4. Foundation First
58	
59	Nothing can be built until the foundation exists: global CSS with the target site's design tokens (colors, fonts, spacing), TypeScript types for the content structures, and global assets (fonts, favicons). This is sequential and non-negotiable. Everything after this can be parallel.
60	
61	### 5. Extract How It Looks AND How It Behaves
62	
63	A website is not a screenshot — it's a living thing. Elements move, change, appear, and disappear in response to scrolling, hovering, clicking, resizing, and time. If you only extract the static CSS of each element, your clone will look right in a screenshot but feel dead when someone actually uses it.
64	
65	For every element, extract its **appearance** (exact computed CSS via `getComputedStyle()`) AND its **behavior** (what changes, what triggers the change, and how the transition happens). Not "it looks like 16px" — extract the actual computed value. Not "the nav changes on scroll" — document the exact trigger (scroll position, IntersectionObserver threshold, viewport intersection), the before and after states (both sets of CSS values), and the transition (duration, easing, CSS transition vs. JS-driven vs. CSS `animation-timeline`).
66	
67	Examples of behaviors to watch for — these are illustrative, not exhaustive. The page may do things not on this list, and you must catch those too:
68	- A navbar that shrinks, changes background, or gains a shadow after scrolling past a threshold
69	- Elements that animate into view when they enter the viewport (fade-up, slide-in, stagger delays)
70	- Sections that snap into place on scroll (`scroll-snap-type`)
71	- Parallax layers that move at different rates than the scroll
72	- Hover states that animate (not just change — the transition duration and easing matter)
73	- Dropdowns, modals, accordions with enter/exit animations
74	- Scroll-driven progress indicators or opacity transitions
75	- Auto-playing carousels or cycling content
76	- Dark-to-light (or any theme) transitions between page sections
77	- **Tabbed/pill content that cycles** — buttons that switch visible card sets with transitions
78	- **Scroll-driven tab/accordion switching** — sidebars where the active item auto-changes as content scrolls past (IntersectionObserver, NOT click handlers)
79	- **Smooth scroll libraries** (Lenis, Locomotive Scroll) — check for `.lenis` class or scroll container wrappers
80	
81	### 6. Identify the Interaction Model Before Building
82	
83	This is the single most expensive mistake in cloning: building a click-based UI when the original is scroll-driven, or vice versa. Before writing any builder prompt for an interactive section, you must definitively answer: **Is this section driven by clicks, scrolls, hovers, time, or some combination?**
84	
85	How to determine this:
86	1. **Don't click first.** Scroll through the section slowly and observe if things change on their own as you scroll.
87	2. If they do, it's scroll-driven. Extract the mechanism: `IntersectionObserver`, `scroll-snap`, `position: sticky`, `animation-timeline`, or JS scroll listeners.
88	3. If nothing changes on scroll, THEN click/hover to test for click/hover-driven interactivity.
89	4. Document the interaction model explicitly in the component spec: "INTERACTION MODEL: scroll-driven with IntersectionObserver" or "INTERACTION MODEL: click-to-switch with opacity transition."
90	
91	A section with a sticky sidebar and scrolling content panels is fundamentally different from a tabbed interface where clicking switches content. Getting this wrong means a complete rewrite, not a CSS tweak.
92	
93	### 7. Extract Every State, Not Just the Default
94	
95	Many components have multiple visual states — a tab bar shows different cards per tab, a header looks different at scroll position 0 vs 100, a card has hover effects. You must extract ALL states, not just whatever is visible on page load.
96	
97	For tabbed/stateful content:
98	- Click each tab/button via browser MCP
99	- Extract the content, images, and card data for EACH state
100	- Record which content belongs to which state
101	- Note the transition animation between states (opacity, slide, fade, etc.)
102	
103	For scroll-dependent elements:
104	- Capture computed styles at scroll position 0 (initial state)
105	- Scroll past the trigger threshold and capture computed styles again (scrolled state)
106	- Diff the two to identify exactly which CSS properties change
107	- Record the transition CSS (duration, easing, properties)
108	- Record the exact trigger threshold (scroll position in px, or viewport intersection ratio)
109	
110	### 8. Spec Files Are the Source of Truth
111	
112	Every component gets a specification file in `docs/research/components/` BEFORE any builder is dispatched. This file is the contract between your extraction work and the builder agent. The builder receives the spec file contents inline in its prompt — the file also persists as an auditable artifact that the user (or you) can review if something looks wrong.
113	
114	The spec file is not optional. It is not a nice-to-have. If you dispatch a builder without first writing a spec file, you are shipping incomplete instructions based on whatever you can remember from a browser MCP session, and the builder will guess to fill gaps.
115	
116	### 9. Build Must Always Compile
117	
118	Every builder agent must verify `npx tsc --noEmit` passes before finishing. After merging worktrees, you verify `npm run build` passes. A broken build is never acceptable, even temporarily.
119	
120	## Phase 1: Reconnaissance
121	
122	Navigate to the target URL with browser MCP.
123	
124	### Screenshots
125	- Take **full-page screenshots** at desktop (1440px) and mobile (390px) viewports
126	- Save to `docs/design-references/` with descriptive names
127	- These are your master reference — builders will receive section-specific crops/screenshots later
128	
129	### Global Extraction
130	Extract these from the page before doing anything else:
131	
132	**Fonts** — Inspect `<link>` tags for Google Fonts or self-hosted fonts. Check computed `font-family` on key elements (headings, body, code, labels). Document every family, weight, and style actually used. Configure them in `src/app/layout.tsx` using `next/font/google` or `next/font/local`.
133	
134	**Colors** — Extract the site's color palette from computed styles across the page. Update `src/app/globals.css` with the target's actual colors in the `:root` and `.dark` CSS variable blocks. Map them to shadcn's token names (background, foreground, primary, muted, etc.) where they fit. Add custom properties for colors that don't map to shadcn tokens.
135	
136	**Favicons & Meta** — Download favicons, apple-touch-icons, OG images, webmanifest to `public/seo/`. Update `layout.tsx` metadata.
137	
138	**Global UI patterns** — Identify any site-wide CSS or JS: custom scrollbar hiding, scroll-snap on the page container, global keyframe animations, backdrop filters, gradients used as overlays, **smooth scroll libraries** (Lenis, Locomotive Scroll — check for `.lenis`, `.locomotive-scroll`, or custom scroll container classes). Add these to `globals.css` and note any libraries that need to be installed.
139	
140	### Mandatory Interaction Sweep
141	
142	This is a dedicated pass AFTER screenshots and BEFORE anything else. Its purpose is to discover every behavior on the page — many of which are invisible in a static screenshot.
143	
144	**Scroll sweep:** Scroll the page slowly from top to bottom via browser MCP. At each section, pause and observe:
145	- Does the header change appearance? Record the scroll position where it triggers.
146	- Do elements animate into view? Record which ones and the animation type.
147	- Does a sidebar or tab indicator auto-switch as you scroll? Record the mechanism.
148	- Are there scroll-snap points? Record which containers.
149	- Is there a smooth scroll library active? Check for non-native scroll behavior.
150	
151	**Click sweep:** Click every element that looks interactive:
152	- Every button, tab, pill, link, card
153	- Record what happens: does content change? Does a modal open? Does a dropdown appear?
154	- For tabs/pills: click EACH ONE and record the content that appears for each state
155	
156	**Hover sweep:** Hover over every element that might have hover states:
157	- Buttons, cards, links, images, nav items
158	- Record what changes: color, scale, shadow, underline, opacity
159	
160	**Responsive sweep:** Test at 3 viewport widths via browser MCP:
161	- Desktop: 1440px
162	- Tablet: 768px
163	- Mobile: 390px
164	- At each width, note which sections change layout (column → stack, sidebar disappears, etc.) and at approximately which breakpoint the change occurs.
165	
166	Save all findings to `docs/research/BEHAVIORS.md`. This is your behavior bible — reference it when writing every component spec.
167	
168	### Page Topology
169	Map out every distinct section of the page from top to bottom. Give each a working name. Document:
170	- Their visual order
171	- Which are fixed/sticky overlays vs. flow content
172	- The overall page layout (scroll container, column structure, z-index layers)
173	- Dependencies between sections (e.g., a floating nav that overlays everything)
174	- **The interaction model** of each section (static, click-driven, scroll-driven, time-driven)
175	
176	Save this as `docs/research/PAGE_TOPOLOGY.md` — it becomes your assembly blueprint.
177	
178	## Phase 2: Foundation Build
179	
180	This is sequential. Do it yourself (not delegated to an agent) since it touches many files:
181	
182	1. **Update fonts** in `layout.tsx` to match the target site's actual fonts
183	2. **Update globals.css** with the target's color tokens, spacing values, keyframe animations, utility classes, and any **global scroll behaviors** (Lenis, smooth scroll CSS, scroll-snap on body)
184	3. **Create TypeScript interfaces** in `src/types/` for the content structures you've observed
185	4. **Extract SVG icons** — find all inline `<svg>` elements on the page, deduplicate them, and save as named React components in `src/components/icons.tsx`. Name them by visual function (e.g., `SearchIcon`, `ArrowRightIcon`, `LogoIcon`).
186	5. **Download global assets** — write and run a Node.js script (`scripts/download-assets.mjs`) that downloads all images, videos, and other binary assets from the page to `public/`. Preserve meaningful directory structure.
187	6. Verify: `npm run build` passes
188	
189	### Asset Discovery Script Pattern
190	
191	Use browser MCP to enumerate all assets on the page:
192	
193	```javascript
194	// Run this via browser MCP to discover all assets
195	JSON.stringify({
196	  images: [...document.querySelectorAll('img')].map(img => ({
197	    src: img.src || img.currentSrc,
198	    alt: img.alt,
199	    width: img.naturalWidth,
200	    height: img.naturalHeight,
201	    // Include parent info to detect layered compositions
202	    parentClasses: img.parentElement?.className,
203	    siblings: img.parentElement ? [...img.parentElement.querySelectorAll('img')].length : 0,
204	    position: getComputedStyle(img).position,
205	    zIndex: getComputedStyle(img).zIndex
206	  })),
207	  videos: [...document.querySelectorAll('video')].map(v => ({
208	    src: v.src || v.querySelector('source')?.src,
209	    poster: v.poster,
210	    autoplay: v.autoplay,
211	    loop: v.loop,
212	    muted: v.muted
213	  })),
214	  backgroundImages: [...document.querySelectorAll('*')].filter(el => {
215	    const bg = getComputedStyle(el).backgroundImage;
216	    return bg && bg !== 'none';
217	  }).map(el => ({
218	    url: getComputedStyle(el).backgroundImage,
219	    element: el.tagName + '.' + el.className?.split(' ')[0]
220	  })),
221	  svgCount: document.querySelectorAll('svg').length,
222	  fonts: [...new Set([...document.querySelectorAll('*')].slice(0, 200).map(el => getComputedStyle(el).fontFamily))],
223	  favicons: [...document.querySelectorAll('link[rel*="icon"]')].map(l => ({ href: l.href, sizes: l.sizes?.toString() }))
224	});
225	```
226	
227	Then write a download script that fetches everything to `public/`. Use batched parallel downloads (4 at a time) with proper error handling.
228	
229	## Phase 3: Component Specification & Dispatch
230	
231	This is the core loop. For each section in your page topology (top to bottom), you do THREE things: **extract**, **write the spec file**, then **dispatch builders**.
232	
233	### Step 1: Extract
234	
235	For each section, use browser MCP to extract everything:
236	
237	1. **Screenshot** the section in isolation (scroll to it, screenshot the viewport). Save to `docs/design-references/`.
238	
239	2. **Extract CSS** for every element in the section. Use the extraction script below — don't hand-measure individual properties. Run it once per component container and capture the full output:
240	
241	```javascript
242	// Per-component extraction — run via browser MCP
243	// Replace SELECTOR with the actual CSS selector for the component
244	(function(selector) {
245	  const el = document.querySelector(selector);
246	  if (!el) return JSON.stringify({ error: 'Element not found: ' + selector });
247	  const props = [
248	    'fontSize','fontWeight','fontFamily','lineHeight','letterSpacing','color',
249	    'textTransform','textDecoration','backgroundColor','background',
250	    'padding','paddingTop','paddingRight','paddingBottom','paddingLeft',
251	    'margin','marginTop','marginRight','marginBottom','marginLeft',
252	    'width','height','maxWidth','minWidth','maxHeight','minHeight',
253	    'display','flexDirection','justifyContent','alignItems','gap',
254	    'gridTemplateColumns','gridTemplateRows',
255	    'borderRadius','border','borderTop','borderBottom','borderLeft','borderRight',
256	    'boxShadow','overflow','overflowX','overflowY',
257	    'position','top','right','bottom','left','zIndex',
258	    'opacity','transform','transition','cursor',
259	    'objectFit','objectPosition','mixBlendMode','filter','backdropFilter',
260	    'whiteSpace','textOverflow','WebkitLineClamp'
261	  ];
262	  function extractStyles(element) {
263	    const cs = getComputedStyle(element);
264	    const styles = {};
265	    props.forEach(p => { const v = cs[p]; if (v && v !== 'none' && v !== 'normal' && v !== 'auto' && v !== '0px' && v !== 'rgba(0, 0, 0, 0)') styles[p] = v; });
266	    return styles;
267	  }
268	  function walk(element, depth) {
269	    if (depth > 4) return null;
270	    const children = [...element.children];
271	    return {
272	      tag: element.tagName.toLowerCase(),
273	      classes: element.className?.toString().split(' ').slice(0, 5).join(' '),
274	      text: element.childNodes.length === 1 && element.childNodes[0].nodeType === 3 ? element.textContent.trim().slice(0, 200) : null,
275	      styles: extractStyles(element),
276	      images: element.tagName === 'IMG' ? { src: element.src, alt: element.alt, naturalWidth: element.naturalWidth, naturalHeight: element.naturalHeight } : null,
277	      childCount: children.length,
278	      children: children.slice(0, 20).map(c => walk(c, depth + 1)).filter(Boolean)
279	    };
280	  }
281	  return JSON.stringify(walk(el, 0), null, 2);
282	})('SELECTOR');
283	```
284	
285	3. **Extract multi-state styles** — for any element with multiple states (scroll-triggered, hover, active tab), capture BOTH states:
286	
287	```javascript
288	// State A: capture styles at current state (e.g., scroll position 0)
289	// Then trigger the state change (scroll, click, hover via browser MCP)
290	// State B: re-run the extraction script on the same element
291	// The diff between A and B IS the behavior specification
292	```
293	
294	Record the diff explicitly: "Property X changes from VALUE_A to VALUE_B, triggered by TRIGGER, with transition: TRANSITION_CSS."
295	
296	4. **Extract real content** — all text, alt attributes, aria labels, placeholder text. Use `element.textContent` for each text node. For tabbed/stateful content, **click each tab and extract content per state**.
297	
298	5. **Identify assets** this section uses — which downloaded images/videos from `public/`, which icon components from `icons.tsx`. Check for **layered images** (multiple `<img>` or background-images stacked in the same container).
299	
300	6. **Assess complexity** — how many distinct sub-components does this section contain? A distinct sub-component is an element with its own unique styling, structure, and behavior (e.g., a card, a nav item, a search panel).
301	
302	### Step 2: Write the Component Spec File
303	
304	For each section (or sub-component, if you're breaking it up), create a spec file in `docs/research/components/`. This is NOT optional — every builder must have a corresponding spec file.
305	
306	**File path:** `docs/research/components/<component-name>.spec.md`
307	
308	**Template:**
309	
310	```markdown
311	# <ComponentName> Specification
312	
313	## Overview
314	- **Target file:** `src/components/<ComponentName>.tsx`
315	- **Screenshot:** `docs/design-references/<screenshot-name>.png`
316	- **Interaction model:** <static | click-driven | scroll-driven | time-driven>
317	
318	## DOM Structure
319	<Describe the element hierarchy — what contains what>
320	
321	## Computed Styles (exact values from getComputedStyle)
322	
323	### Container
324	- display: ...
325	- padding: ...
326	- maxWidth: ...
327	- (every relevant property with exact values)
328	
329	### <Child element 1>
330	- fontSize: ...
331	- color: ...
332	- (every relevant property)
333	
334	### <Child element N>
335	...
336	
337	## States & Behaviors
338	
339	### <Behavior name, e.g., "Scroll-triggered floating mode">
340	- **Trigger:** <exact mechanism — scroll position 50px, IntersectionObserver rootMargin "-30% 0px", click on .tab-button, hover>
341	- **State A (before):** maxWidth: 100vw, boxShadow: none, borderRadius: 0
342	- **State B (after):** maxWidth: 1200px, boxShadow: 0 4px 20px rgba(0,0,0,0.1), borderRadius: 16px
343	- **Transition:** transition: all 0.3s ease
344	- **Implementation approach:** <CSS transition + scroll listener | IntersectionObserver | CSS animation-timeline | etc.>
345	
346	### Hover states
347	- **<Element>:** <property>: <before> → <after>, transition: <value>
348	
349	## Per-State Content (if applicable)
350	
351	### State: "Featured"
352	- Title: "..."
353	- Subtitle: "..."
354	- Cards: [{ title, description, image, link }, ...]
355	
356	### State: "Productivity"
357	- Title: "..."
358	- Cards: [...]
359	
360	## Assets
361	- Background image: `public/images/<file>.webp`
362	- Overlay image: `public/images/<file>.png`
363	- Icons used: <ArrowIcon>, <SearchIcon> from icons.tsx
364	
365	## Text Content (verbatim)
366	<All text content, copy-pasted from the live site>
367	
368	## Responsive Behavior
369	- **Desktop (1440px):** <layout description>
370	- **Tablet (768px):** <what changes — e.g., "maintains 2-column, gap reduces to 16px">
371	- **Mobile (390px):** <what changes — e.g., "stacks to single column, images full-width">
372	- **Breakpoint:** layout switches at ~<N>px
373	```
374	
375	Fill every section. If a section doesn't apply (e.g., no states for a static footer), write "N/A" — but think twice before marking States & Behaviors as N/A. Even a footer might have hover states on links.
376	
377	### Step 3: Dispatch Builders
378	
379	Based on complexity, dispatch builder agent(s) in worktree(s):
380	
381	**Simple section** (1-2 sub-components): One builder agent gets the entire section.
382	
383	**Complex section** (3+ distinct sub-components): Break it up. One agent per sub-component, plus one agent for the section wrapper that imports them. Sub-component builders go first since the wrapper depends on them.
384	
385	**What every builder agent receives:**
386	- The full contents of its component spec file (inline in the prompt — don't say "go read the spec file")
387	- Path to the section screenshot in `docs/design-references/`
388	- Which shared components to import (`icons.tsx`, `cn()`, shadcn primitives)
389	- The target file path (e.g., `src/components/HeroSection.tsx`)
390	- Instruction to verify with `npx tsc --noEmit` before finishing
391	- For responsive behavior: the specific breakpoint values and what changes
392	
393	**Don't wait.** As soon as you've dispatched the builder(s) for one section, move to extracting the next section. Builders work in parallel in their worktrees while you continue extraction.
394	
395	### Step 4: Merge
396	
397	As builder agents complete their work:
398	- Merge their worktree branches into main
399	- You have full context on what each agent built, so resolve any conflicts intelligently
400	- After each merge, verify the build still passes: `npm run build`
401	- If a merge introduces type errors, fix them immediately
402	
403	The extract → spec → dispatch → merge cycle continues until all sections are built.
404	
405	## Phase 4: Page Assembly
406	
407	After all sections are built and merged, wire everything together in `src/app/page.tsx`:
408	
409	- Import all section components
410	- Implement the page-level layout from your topology doc (scroll containers, column structures, sticky positioning, z-index layering)
411	- Connect real content to component props
412	- Implement page-level behaviors: scroll snap, scroll-driven animations, dark-to-light transitions, intersection observers, smooth scroll (Lenis etc.)
413	- Verify: `npm run build` passes clean
414	
415	## Phase 5: Visual QA Diff
416	
417	After assembly, do NOT declare the clone complete. Take side-by-side comparison screenshots:
418	
419	1. Open the original site and your clone side-by-side (or take screenshots at the same viewport widths)
420	2. Compare section by section, top to bottom, at desktop (1440px)
421	3. Compare again at mobile (390px)
422	4. For each discrepancy found:
423	   - Check the component spec file — was the value extracted correctly?
424	   - If the spec was wrong: re-extract from browser MCP, update the spec, fix the component
425	   - If the spec was right but the builder got it wrong: fix the component to match the spec
426	5. Test all interactive behaviors: scroll through the page, click every button/tab, hover over interactive elements
427	6. Verify smooth scroll feels right, header transitions work, tab switching works, animations play
428	
429	Only after this visual QA pass is the clone complete.
430	
431	## Pre-Dispatch Checklist
432	
433	Before dispatching ANY builder agent, verify you can check every box. If you can't, go back and extract more.
434	
435	- [ ] Spec file written to `docs/research/components/<name>.spec.md` with ALL sections filled
436	- [ ] Every CSS value in the spec is from `getComputedStyle()`, not estimated
437	- [ ] Interaction model is identified and documented (static / click / scroll / time)
438	- [ ] For stateful components: every state's content and styles are captured
439	- [ ] For scroll-driven components: trigger threshold, before/after styles, and transition are recorded
440	- [ ] For hover states: before/after values and transition timing are recorded
441	- [ ] All images in the section are identified (including overlays and layered compositions)
442	- [ ] Responsive behavior is documented for at least desktop and mobile
443	- [ ] Text content is verbatim from the site, not paraphrased
444	- [ ] The builder prompt is under ~150 lines of spec; if over, the section needs to be split
445	
446	## What NOT to Do
447	
448	These are lessons from previous failed clones — each one cost hours of rework:
449	
450	- **Don't build click-based tabs when the original is scroll-driven (or vice versa).** Determine the interaction model FIRST by scrolling before clicking. This is the #1 most expensive mistake — it requires a complete rewrite, not a CSS fix.
451	- **Don't extract only the default state.** If there are tabs showing "Featured" on load, click Productivity, Creative, Lifestyle and extract each one's cards/content. If the header changes on scroll, capture styles at position 0 AND position 100+.
452	- **Don't miss overlay/layered images.** A background watercolor + foreground UI mockup = 2 images. Check every container's DOM tree for multiple `<img>` elements and positioned overlays.
453	- **Don't build mockup components for content that's actually videos/animations.** Check if a section uses `<video>`, Lottie, or canvas before building elaborate HTML mockups of what the video shows.
454	- **Don't approximate CSS classes.** "It looks like `text-lg`" is wrong if the computed value is `18px` and `text-lg` is `18px/28px` but the actual line-height is `24px`. Extract exact values.
455	- **Don't build everything in one monolithic commit.** The whole point of this pipeline is incremental progress with verified builds at each step.
456	- **Don't reference docs from builder prompts.** Each builder gets the CSS spec inline in its prompt — never "see DESIGN_TOKENS.md for colors." The builder should have zero need to read external docs.
457	- **Don't skip asset extraction.** Without real images, videos, and fonts, the clone will always look fake regardless of how perfect the CSS is.
458	- **Don't give a builder agent too much scope.** If you're writing a builder prompt and it's getting long because the section is complex, that's a signal to break it into smaller tasks.
459	- **Don't bundle unrelated sections into one agent.** A CTA section and a footer are different components with different designs — don't hand them both to one agent and hope for the best.
460	- **Don't skip responsive extraction.** If you only inspect at desktop width, the clone will break at tablet and mobile. Test at 1440, 768, and 390 during extraction.
461	- **Don't forget smooth scroll libraries.** Check for Lenis (`.lenis` class), Locomotive Scroll, or similar. Default browser scrolling feels noticeably different and the user will spot it immediately.
462	- **Don't dispatch builders without a spec file.** The spec file forces exhaustive extraction and creates an auditable artifact. Skipping it means the builder gets whatever you can fit in a prompt from memory.
463	
464	## Completion
465	
466	When done, report:
467	- Total sections built
468	- Total components created
469	- Total spec files written (should match components)
470	- Total assets downloaded (images, videos, SVGs, fonts)
471	- Build status (`npm run build` result)
472	- Visual QA results (any remaining discrepancies)
473	- Any known gaps or limitations
474
```
</details></tool-use>

Now I have the full picture. Here's the answer:

---

## Why `ai-website-cloner-template` appears to support Chrome MCP (but doesn't actually have it either)

**The difference is skill design, not MCP availability.** Neither project currently has Chrome MCP configured.

### Skill pre-flight comparison

| | `clone-to-astro` | `ai-website-cloner-template` |
|---|---|---|
| Browser requirement | **"Chrome MCP is required. If unavailable, STOP."** | **"Check for available browser tools. If none detected, ASK the user."** |
| Fail behavior | Hard stop | Soft ask |
| Specificity | Chrome MCP only | Any: Chrome MCP, Playwright, Puppeteer, Browserbase |

The `ai-website-cloner-template` skill was written to be resilient — it accepts any browser tool and gracefully asks rather than stopping. The `clone-to-astro` skill was written stricter.

---

### What IS installed on your machine

- **`~/.claude/chrome/chrome-native-host.bat`** — This is the **Claude Code Chrome extension bridge** (lets you open Claude Code from the browser). It is NOT a Chrome MCP server for browser automation.
- **No browser MCP server configured** anywhere — not in `claude_desktop_config.json`, not in any project `.mcp.json`.

---

### To fix `clone-to-astro` (and both skills)

Add a browser MCP server. The easiest option is Puppeteer MCP. Run this in the `clone-to-astro` directory:

```bash
! claude mcp add puppeteer -- npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-puppeteer
```

Or add it to `claude_desktop_config.json`:

```json
"mcpServers": {
  "puppeteer": {
    "command": "npx",
    "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-puppeteer"]
  }
}
```

Then restart Claude Code and re-run `/clone-to-astro https://www.beyond-vietnam.com`.

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-16 17:07:22Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-16 17:07:29Z)**_

For the `clone-to-astro` use case (screenshots, `getComputedStyle`, DOM inspection, JavaScript execution on a live site), here's the comparison:

## Puppeteer MCP vs Playwright MCP

| | Puppeteer | Playwright |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `@modelcontextprotocol/server-puppeteer` | `@playwright/mcp` |
| Maintained by | Model Context Protocol (community) | Microsoft (official) |
| Browser support | Chrome/Chromium only | Chromium, Firefox, WebKit |
| Screenshot quality | Good | Excellent (full-page, element-level) |
| JS execution | Yes (`evaluate`) | Yes (`evaluate`) |
| `getComputedStyle` | Yes | Yes |
| Headless mode | Yes | Yes |
| Active development | Slower | Very active |
| Stability | Mature | More modern, better error handling |

## Recommendation: **Playwright MCP**

For this cloning task specifically:

1. **Official Microsoft support** — `@playwright/mcp` is the official MCP server, actively maintained
2. **Better screenshot tools** — supports full-page screenshots and element-scoped screenshots natively
3. **More reliable JS execution** — better timeout handling and error reporting
4. **Viewport control** — easier to switch between 1440px / 768px / 390px for responsive extraction
5. **Richer tool set** — exposes more granular browser actions out of the box

## Setup

```bash
claude mcp add playwright -- npx @playwright/mcp@latest
```

Or add to `claude_desktop_config.json`:

```json
"mcpServers": {
  "playwright": {
    "command": "npx",
    "args": ["@playwright/mcp@latest"]
  }
}
```

Then restart Claude Code and run `/clone-to-astro https://www.beyond-vietnam.com`.

so I can install the playwright mcp global and use in every workspace

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-16 17:09:10Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-04-16 17:09:13Z)**_

Yes. Add it to your user-level Claude Code settings and it will be available in every workspace.

## Option 1: Via CLI (recommended)

```bash
claude mcp add --scope user playwright -- npx @playwright/mcp@latest
```

The `--scope user` flag writes it to `~/.claude/settings.json` instead of a project-level `.mcp.json`.

## Option 2: Manually edit `~/.claude/settings.json`

Your settings file is at `C:\Users\hung.dang\.claude\settings.json`. Add an `mcpServers` block:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@playwright/mcp@latest"]
    }
  }
}
```

---

After adding it, restart Claude Code. Playwright MCP tools will then appear in every project/workspace you open — including `clone-to-astro`.
